body.content:
Copenhagen didn't become the world's best bike city because Danes are intrinsically virtuous. It became the world's best bike city because a series of unglamorous policy decisions, made over forty years, turned out to be right.

## The chain of small choices

- Separated lanes, not paint. A line on the road tells a driver nothing. A curb tells them everything.
- Bridges for bikes first. The Cykelslangen — the "bicycle snake" — was built before its car equivalent.
- Snow plowed from bike lanes *before* car lanes during winter storms. That single policy is worth more than any campaign.

## What it gives back

A modal share above 49% for trips to work or school. A reduction in healthcare costs estimated at €235M per year. And a quality of urban life that anyone who has spent a week there knows in their bones.

The lesson isn't that other cities should copy Copenhagen. It's that infrastructure compounds — quietly, over decades — into culture.

cardInfo.summary: How forty years of unglamorous policy turned Copenhagen into the world's bike capital — and what that means for everyone else.

attributes.headline: The Bike City: How Copenhagen Quietly Won

featuredImage.altText: A row of cyclists crossing a bridge during morning rush in Copenhagen.
